sections.heading: Overview
sections.content: The management of multiple myeloma (MM) and related plasma cell disorders involves a risk-adapted, multimodal approach. Treatment decisions are guided by disease stage (e.g., solitary plasmacytoma, smoldering myeloma, symptomatic MM), risk stratification (e.g., ISS, R-ISS, R2-ISS, IMS-IMWG), patient factors (age, performance status, frailty, comorbidities), and molecular characteristics (cytogenetic abnormalities, MRD status). The primary goal for symptomatic MM is to achieve deep and durable responses, while minimizing toxicity. Supportive care, including bone-modifying agents, infection prophylaxis, and management of complications (renal impairment, hypercalcemia, VTE), is integral to improving outcomes. For asymptomatic conditions like smoldering myeloma, observation or early intervention based on risk is considered. Clinical trial participation is encouraged for all eligible patients. The NCCN Panel emphasizes equitable and inclusive clinical trial design to address disparities, particularly in Black/African American individuals who have higher myeloma incidence and distinct biological features [MYEL-F].

sections.heading: MDT Requirements
sections.content: Treatment recommendations should be made after joint consultation and/or discussion by a multidisciplinary team including hematologists/oncologists, radiation oncologists, surgeons (for plasmacytomas or fractures), radiologists, pathologists, and palliative care specialists. For complex cases like CNS disease, involvement of neurologists and neurosurgeons is advised [MYEL-D, MYEL-H].

sections.heading: Performance Status Guide
sections.content: Frailty assessment should be considered in older adults using tools like the IMWG Myeloma Frailty Score Calculator. Performance status influences regimen selection: frail patients may start with 2- or 3-drug regimens with dose modifications, and steroids should be reduced or discontinued based on response and toxicity. Renal dysfunction and advanced age are not contraindications to autologous HCT but may require adjusted dosing [MYEL-F, MYEL-G].

nodes.label: Solitary Plasmacytoma (osseous or extramedullary) with minimal marrow involvement (<10% clonal plasma cells)
Radiation therapy (RT) to the involved site; surgery for structural instability or neurologic compromise
Close surveillance for progression to MM
High-risk: consider clinical trial or early therapy with single-agent daratumumab (cat1) or lenalidomide (cat2B)
HCT-eligible: Primary therapy with quadruplet regimens (e.g., Dara-VRd, Isa-VRd); then autologous HCT; then maintenance therapy
HCT-ineligible or deferred: Regimens like D-Rd, Dara-VRd (for non-frail patients <80 years); then maintenance
Based on prior therapies: triplet regimens, CAR T-cell therapy (cilta-cel, ide-cel), bispecific antibodies (elranatamab, teclistamab), or clinical trials; consider re-challenge if relapse >6 months
Refractory disease with lack of treatment options
Palliative care: symptom management with specialist, supportive care, hospice

edges.label: Solitary plasmacytoma (osseous or extramedullary) with minimal marrow involvement (<10% clonal plasma cells)
Refractory disease with lack of treatment options
Select regimen based on prior therapy

cards.title: Curative intent
cards.detail:
Intent: Curative intent
Patient group: Solitary plasmacytoma (osseous or extramedullary) with minimal marrow involvement (<10% clonal plasma cells)
Approach: Radiation therapy (RT) to the involved site is the intervention of choice. Surgery may be considered for structural instability or neurologic compromise. Close surveillance for progression to MM is required [MYEL-2, MYEL-D].

cards.title: Disease control/prevention of progression
cards.detail:
Intent: Disease control/prevention of progression
Patient group: Smoldering (asymptomatic) myeloma
Approach: Risk stratification using Mayo 2018/IMWG 20/2/20 criteria. Low-risk patients may be observed at 3-6 month intervals. High-risk patients may consider clinical trials or early therapy with single-agent daratumumab (category 1) or lenalidomide (category 2B) [MYEL-3].

cards.title: Remission induction and deep response
cards.detail:
Intent: Remission induction and deep response
Patient group: Symptomatic MM (newly diagnosed)
Approach: Primary therapy with quadruplet regimens preferred for HCT-eligible patients (e.g., Dara-VRd, Isa-VRd). For HCT-ineligible or deferred, regimens like D-Rd, Dara-VRd (for non-frail patients <80 years) are preferred. Autologous HCT is standard for eligible patients. Maintenance therapy post-HCT is recommended [MYEL-4, MYEL-5, MYEL-G].

cards.title: Disease control in relapsed/refractory MM
cards.detail:
Intent: Disease control in relapsed/refractory MM
Patient group: Previously treated MM
Approach: Treatment based on prior therapies, refractory status, and depth of response. Options include triplet regimens, CAR T-cell therapy (e.g., cilta-cel, ide-cel), bispecific antibodies (e.g., elranatamab, teclistamab), or clinical trials. Consider re-challenge with prior agents if relapse after >6 months [MYEL-6, MYEL-G].

cards.title: Palliative care
cards.detail:
Intent: Palliative care
Patient group: Refractory disease with lack of treatment options
Approach: Symptom management with palliative care specialist, supportive care, and hospice initiation as appropriate [MYEL-6].
